title: Subaltern silence: A postcolonial genealogy [Book Review]
author: Mendez, Oberprantacher

opening_move.description: The review opens with Olson's concluding claim that subaltern silence is ubiquitous in the contemporary world, using Mbembe's 'Becoming Black' to establish the stakes: subalternity is not an elsewhere but a present condition.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: How to articulate silence without muting it — the paradox of speaking for/with subalterns without ventriloquism

rivals_engaged: abstraction of Haiti into theoretical model
theoretical_positioning.description: The review endorses Olson's genealogical project but raises a tension: his focus on Haiti-as-paradigm risks abstracting a specific historical-political site into a general theoretical model, suppressing its own epistemic complexity.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: postcolonial genealogy; close reading of Olson and Spivak
epistemic_mode.notes: Book review genre. The central tension raised (paradigm case vs. dynamic historical site) is the reviewers' independent critical contribution.

confidence_notes: Book review genre with substantive immanent critique. The reviewers' concern about theorising from Haiti is the distinctive contribution.
